Pip · The Six · Edition 2

The Moon
and Us

Why humans have always looked up. And why, after fifty years, we are finally going back.

Artemis II flew around the moon in April 2026
What is Artemis? Why does the moon matter? When did we first go? How does a mission work? Where exactly are we headed? Who is in the race?

For curious readers aged 10 to 17 · April 2026

The Six Honest Questions

What and Why and When and How and Where and Who

"I keep six honest serving-men
(They taught me all I knew);
Their names are What and Why and When
And How and Where and Who."

Rudyard Kipling, The Elephant's Child, Just So Stories, 1902

Kipling's six questions are the ones journalists, scientists, and curious children have always asked. They are also the six questions Pip brings to every edition of The Six. Each question gets a page. Together, they tell the whole story.

This time, the questions are about the moon.

What is Artemis, and why did four people just fly around the moon?
Why has the moon held humans in its spell since the beginning of time?
When did we first get there, and what happened in the fifty years after?
How does a mission to the moon actually work?
Where exactly are we going, and why does that spot matter?
Who is in this race, and what does each player want?

Let's find out.

· · ·
The Scene

Four people. Farther from home than any human before them.

On 1 April 2026, a rocket launched from Florida carrying four astronauts. Five days later, they flew around the far side of the moon. It is the side no human had ever seen from that close. No Apollo astronaut ever reached this angle. These four were the first.

During that flyby, they also broke a record. They travelled farther from Earth than any human being has ever been. Further than Neil Armstrong. Further than the Apollo 13 crew, who had held the record since 1970. Further than anyone in history.

The mission is called Artemis II. The crew splash down on 10 April 2026. And to understand why it matters, you have to understand everything that happened before it.

Something to think about

The Apollo 13 crew held the "farthest from Earth" record since 1970. That is longer than most of your parents have been alive. Why do you think it took this long before someone tried to beat it?

Artemis II lifts off, 1 April 2026. The Space Launch System rocket carries Orion and four astronauts away from Kennedy Space Center in Florida. This is the first crewed launch toward the moon in 54 years. Photo: NASA / public domain
The crew of Artemis II
Four people. One historic window.

Commander Reid Wiseman. Pilot Victor Glover. Mission Specialist Christina Koch. And Jeremy Hansen from Canada. Koch is the first woman to fly this deep into space. Glover is the first person of colour to go beyond Earth orbit. Hansen is the first Canadian. All four return to Earth on 10 April 2026.

The numbers
695,081 miles. 10 days. One record broken.

The total distance Artemis II travels from launch to splashdown. Maximum distance from Earth: 252,760 miles. The previous human record, held by the Apollo 13 crew since April 1970, was 248,655 miles.

Why Artemis?

In Greek mythology, Artemis is the twin sister of Apollo. Apollo was the name of the first moon missions. The new programme is named Artemis because it picks up where Apollo ended. And because Artemis will, for the first time, land a woman on the moon.

Page 2 — What
Pip · The Six
What · The Moon and Us 2
What

The thing in the sky that humans have never been able to ignore

Before there was science, there was the moon. It appeared every night. It changed shape, disappeared, and came back. It moved the tides. It lit the darkness. And it was close enough to see clearly, impossibly far to reach.

Every culture in the world looked up at the same moon and told a story about it. The stories are different. The impulse was identical.

In India, the moon is Chandra. A god. Born during the Samudra Manthan, the great churning of the cosmic ocean, from which also came Goddess Lakshmi and the nectar of immortality. Chandra rides a silver chariot pulled by ten white horses. He is the lord of the 27 nakshatras, the lunar constellations. Indian kings traced their lineage to him. The Pandavas of the Mahabharata are descendants of the lunar dynasty. Even Ramchandra, Ram's very name, carries the moon inside it.

The moon's phases explain themselves through Chandra's story. He had 27 wives, one for each nakshatra, but favoured only Rohini. The other wives complained to their father, who cursed Chandra to wane and fade. Lord Shiva partially lifted the curse. And so the moon waxes and wanes, every single month, forever.

For Indian children, the moon is Chandamama. Uncle Moon. In the lullabies, in the bedtime stories, in the children's magazine Chandamama that was published from 1947 until 2013 in thirteen Indian languages and read by generations across the country, the moon is not distant and frightening. The moon is family.

In Japan, a rabbit lives on the moon, grinding rice cakes. In Norse mythology, two children were captured by the moon to carry water forever. In ancient Egypt, Thoth, the moon god, was the god of writing and knowledge. In Islam, the crescent moon is a symbol of divine light, visible in the night sky of every Muslim country's flag.

The moon did not just live in religion. It lived in stories, films, and music. In 1865, Jules Verne wrote a novel about a rocket to the moon that launched from Florida, 101 years before the real thing. In 1902, the filmmaker Georges Méliès made the world's first science fiction film. A rocket flies to the moon. The Man in the Moon gets a rocket in the eye. It is sixteen minutes long and watchable today. In 1950, the Belgian cartoonist Hergé published Destination Moon, a Tintin story about a boy going to the moon in a rocket, nineteen years before Apollo 11 made it real. Hergé consulted real rocket scientists. He got the details right.

Then the Space Age arrived, and the moon shifted from mythology to mission.

Something to think about

India calls the moon Chandamama. Uncle Moon. Other cultures gave the moon a god, a rabbit, a face. Why do you think humans everywhere felt the need to turn the moon into a character?

Two moons, one sky. On the left: Chandra, the moon god of Indian mythology, riding his chariot across the heavens. The text beside him reads चंदमामा, Chandamama, Uncle Moon. On the right: the scientific moon, cold and cratered, as seen through a modern telescope. The same object. Two thousand years of human understanding, side by side. Illustration: Pip / The Six
Rabbit holes
Watch and explore

The 1902 Méliès film is on YouTube, completely free. It is 14 minutes long and unlike anything you have seen before.

Watch: A Trip to the Moon (1902)

Tintin on the moon predates Apollo by nearly 20 years. The two albums are Destination Moon (1953) and Explorers on the Moon (1954).

Read: Tintin's moon mission
🎬 James Bond: Moonraker (1979)
A villain plans to escape to a space station and repopulate Earth from orbit. Released just ten years after Apollo 11, when space was everywhere in popular culture. More about the film
🎵 Pink Floyd: The Dark Side of the Moon (1973)
One of the best-selling albums in history. More than 45 million copies sold. The title and artwork were inspired by the Apollo era. Read more
📖 Chandamama magazine (1947–2013)
Published for 66 years in 13 Indian languages. Beloved by generations of Indian children. Its name was the moon itself. History of Chandamama
📚 From the Earth to the Moon (Jules Verne, 1865)
Written 104 years before Apollo. The fictional rocket launched from Florida. Verne's accuracy has amazed readers ever since. Read more
India connection

The Rigveda, one of the world's oldest surviving texts, sings of Chandra. Indian kings for centuries traced their lineage to the moon. The very word "Monday" comes from "Moon's day," a concept shared across cultures. In Sanskrit, Somavar, Monday, is Soma's day. Soma is another name for Chandra.

Page 3 — Where and Why
Pip · The Six
Where / Why · The Moon and Us 3
Where and Why

384,400 kilometres away. And worth going back to.

The moon is 384,400 kilometres from Earth. That sounds enormous. But in space terms, it is next door. The sun is 400 times further away. The nearest star after our sun is 100 million times further. The moon is practically in our backyard.

How far is 384,400 km, really?
If you lined up 30 planet Earths in a row, they would just fit in the gap between the Earth and the Moon. Here they are:
🌍
🌕
🌍🌍🌍🌍🌍 🌍🌍🌍🌍🌍 🌍🌍🌍🌍🌍 🌍🌍🌍🌍🌍 🌍🌍🌍🌍🌍 🌍🌍🌍🌍🌍
Those are 30 planet Earths, side by side. That is the gap you would need to cross. At 100 km/h without stopping, the drive would take 160 days. A spacecraft travels at around 39,000 km/h, so the trip takes about 4 days.

The moon is about a quarter the size of Earth. Its diameter is roughly the same as the width of Australia. It has no atmosphere, no weather, and no liquid water on the surface. Temperatures swing from 127 degrees Celsius in sunlight to minus 173 in shadow. Nothing lives there.

But something remarkable is hiding in the shadows near the south pole. Ice. Frozen water, buried in craters where sunlight never reaches. These craters have been in permanent shadow for billions of years.

This is not a small detail. Water can be split into hydrogen and oxygen. Hydrogen and oxygen are rocket fuel. A fuel station on the moon changes everything. Missions to Mars, which would currently need to carry all their fuel from Earth, could refuel at the moon instead. The south pole is not just a scientific destination. It is a potential petrol station for the rest of the solar system.

There is another reason countries are paying close attention. The south pole is believed to hold rare minerals and helium-3, a material that could power nuclear fusion reactors. Countries that get there first will have first claim on what they find.

If you read Edition 1 of The Six, about oil and the Middle East, you may notice something familiar here.

The moon's south pole: where the next chapter begins. The dark craters marked here have not seen sunlight in billions of years. That permanent shadow is why they matter. Frozen water ice sits undisturbed inside them. Shiv Shakti Point, where India's Chandrayaan-3 landed in August 2023, sits near the Artemis target zone. Both programmes are headed to the same neighbourhood. Illustration: Pip / The Six
India was here first

In August 2023, ISRO's Chandrayaan-3 became the first spacecraft to successfully land near the moon's south pole. It found evidence of sulphur, iron, oxygen, and other elements in the soil. The landing site was named "Shiv Shakti Point." The data from Chandrayaan-3 is directly shaping where Artemis plans to land. India did not just enter this race. India helped define the finish line.

Rabbit hole
See the south pole

NASA's Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter has mapped every crater near the south pole. The images are remarkable.

Explore: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter
Why not the equator this time?

All six Apollo landings happened near the moon's equator, in flat sunlit areas safe for 1960s technology. The south pole is harder to reach and far darker. But the ice there is the prize. No human has ever visited the south pole of the moon. Artemis III aims to change that.

Page 4 — When and How (History)
Pip · The Six
When / How · The Moon and Us 4
When and How — The History

We went. We stopped. We forgot. Now we are going back.

The story of humans and the moon has three chapters. The race. The pause. The return.

Chapter one started in 1957, when the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first satellite. It was the size of a beach ball. It beeped as it orbited Earth. Americans were alarmed. If the Soviets could put something in space, they could put a weapon anywhere on Earth.

In 1961, US President John F. Kennedy made a promise: America would put a man on the moon before the end of the decade. He chose those words carefully. He said it should be done "because it is hard." This was not a science project. It was a political statement in a Cold War between two superpowers.

Eight years later, on 20 July 1969, Neil Armstrong stepped onto the moon. He said: "That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind." 600 million people watched on television. That was one in five of every person alive on Earth.

  • 1969 — Apollo 11
    Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walk on the moon. Michael Collins orbits above. The Space Race is decided.
  • 1969–1972
    Six missions land. Twelve astronauts walk on the surface. Apollo 13 fails mid-mission but the crew survive by using the lunar lander as a lifeboat — one of the greatest rescues in history.
  • 1972 — Apollo 17
    The last time humans walked on the moon. Commander Gene Cernan scratches his daughter Tracy's initials in the lunar dust before climbing back in. He is the last person to stand there.
  • 1972–2026 — The long pause
    No human leaves Earth orbit. Three separate US programmes to return to the moon are proposed and cancelled. Fifty-four years pass.
  • November 2022 — Artemis I
    An uncrewed test flight of the Orion spacecraft loops around the moon. All systems work. The path to a crewed mission opens.
  • April 2026 — Artemis II
    Four astronauts fly around the moon. A record is broken. The story resumes.
The gap — and the conspiracy

Here is the question everyone asks. If humans reached the moon in 1969 with computers less powerful than a modern calculator, why did it take 54 years to go back? This discomfort led some people to a different answer: it never happened. The moon landing hoax theory says NASA staged the Apollo missions in a film studio. It has persisted since the 1970s. The evidence against it is overwhelming. Scientists left 382 kilograms of moon rocks that researchers worldwide have studied. Retroreflectors placed on the surface by Apollo astronauts still bounce laser beams fired from Earth today. The Soviet Union, who desperately wanted America to fail, never claimed the landing was faked. The real reason for the 54-year gap is simpler: Apollo was a sprint. Once America won the Space Race, the urgency vanished. NASA's budget had been more than 4% of the entire US federal budget at the Apollo peak. It fell to under 0.5% within a decade. The engineers who built the Saturn V retired. The production lines shut down. Three separate programmes to return were started and cancelled, one after another, as each new US president changed the plan. The knowledge was not lost. But rebuilding it took decades.

The conspiracy theory even made it into pop music. In 1992, R.E.M. released a song called "Man on the Moon," using the fake-landing idea as a metaphor for the comedian Andy Kaufman, who many people believed had faked his own death. The key lyric: "If you believed they put a man on the moon... if you believe there's nothing up my sleeve, then nothing is cool." The music video included footage from the same 1902 Méliès film linked on the previous page. The moon connects everything, even things that have nothing to do with the moon.

Listen: R.E.M. — Man on the Moon (1992)
Buzz Aldrin on the Sea of Tranquility, 20 July 1969. The American flag behind him stands without wind, held rigid by a horizontal rod, because the moon has no atmosphere. The bootprints in the dust around him are still there today. They will outlast every building on Earth. This photograph was taken by Neil Armstrong, the only Apollo 11 image on which Armstrong himself does not appear. Photo: Neil Armstrong / NASA / public domain
The hoax question
Did we really go?

Yes. One proof: the retroreflectors Apollo astronauts left on the moon can still be pinged with lasers from Earth today. Any observatory with the right equipment can find them. The moon carries our fingerprints.

Read: Moon landing conspiracies, debunked
India in the space age

ISRO was founded in 1969, the same year as Apollo 11. While the US and USSR raced each other to the moon, India was building its first rocket programme from scratch. Today, India is one of only four countries to have successfully landed a spacecraft on the moon: the US, the Soviet Union, China, and India.

What happened to the Saturn V?

The Saturn V rocket that took Apollo astronauts to the moon was never flown after 1973. The design was retired. Some complete Saturn V rockets are on display in US museums today. They are so large that visitors standing beside them often cannot take in the full scale of what they are looking at.

Page 5 — How (Mission Infographic)
Pip · The Six
How · The Moon and Us 5
How

How do you fly to the moon? Step by step.

Everything that happened on Artemis II, in order.

1
Launch — 1 April 2026, 6:35 PM Florida time

The Space Launch System ignites. At liftoff, it generates 8.8 million pounds of thrust. The two solid rocket boosters burn out and fall away within the first two minutes. The main engines push the rocket through the atmosphere for about eight minutes total.

8.8 million lbs thrust 8 minutes to orbit
2
Earth Orbit and Systems Check

Orion enters orbit around Earth. The crew test life support, manual control, and communications. Pilot Victor Glover manually flies Orion around the detached upper stage of the rocket, a 70-minute test drive called proximity operations. They orbit for about a day and a half.

1.5 days in Earth orbit 70-minute manual test
3
Translunar Injection — the point of no return

The engine fires for 5 minutes and 55 seconds. This single burn breaks Orion free of Earth's gravity and sets it on a path to the moon. After this moment, even if the engine never fires again, the moon's gravity will pull Orion around it and send it back to Earth on its own. The crew look at each other. Nobody speaks.

5 min 55 sec burn Free return trajectory begins
4
Deep Space Transit — four days in a campervan

The crew live, sleep, eat, and exercise inside a space about the size of a large campervan. They test medical equipment, practise emergency drills in their spacesuits, check the carbon dioxide scrubbers, and watch the moon grow larger in the window with every hour that passes.

4 days of transit ~16.5 ft wide capsule
5
Lunar Flyby — 6 April 2026, the main event

Orion swings around the far side of the moon. At closest approach, the crew are 4,070 miles from the surface. They photograph 35 specific geological targets. They pass over the Apollo 12 and Apollo 14 landing sites from above. They break the distance-from-Earth record. And for about 30 minutes, the moon blocks all radio signals. They are completely cut off from Earth. No communication in either direction. Alone, in the dark, farther from home than any human has ever been.

4,070 miles from surface 30 min communications blackout Record: 252,760 miles from Earth
6
Return and Splashdown — 10 April 2026

Orion follows the free-return trajectory back to Earth. Re-entry into the atmosphere happens at about 40,000 km/h. The heat shield reaches 2,760 degrees Celsius. Parachutes deploy. Splashdown in the Pacific Ocean, off San Diego. Recovery teams retrieve the crew by helicopter and deliver them to a waiting ship.

40,000 km/h re-entry 2,760°C heat shield Pacific Ocean splashdown
The complete Artemis II flight path, start to finish. The spacecraft launches into Earth orbit (1), fires its engine to break free toward the moon (2: Translunar Injection), completes proximity operations tests (3), swings around the far side of the moon at closest approach (4: Lunar Flyby), coasts back toward Earth (5), and re-enters the atmosphere for splashdown (6). The dotted loop around the moon is the free-return trajectory: even without firing the engine again after the TLI burn, gravity alone would bring the crew home. Illustration: Pip / The Six
Something to think about

During the lunar flyby, the crew lose contact with Earth for about 30 minutes. No radio. No instructions. No support. Completely on their own, farther from home than anyone has ever been. What would you think about in those 30 minutes?

Page 6 — Who
Pip · The Six
Who · The Moon and Us 6
Who

The players — and what they each want

The moon is not a science project anymore. It is a geopolitical destination. Here are the main players.

🇺🇸The United States

NASA is leading the Artemis programme. The goal is not just to visit the moon but to establish long-term human presence there. The Artemis Accords, a set of agreements about peaceful space exploration, have been signed by 50 countries. The US wants to set the rules before anyone else does. Artemis III, the next mission after Artemis II, aims to land at the lunar south pole for the first time in history. It would be the first human landing on the moon since Apollo 17 in December 1972.

🇨🇳China

China has its own lunar programme and has already landed spacecraft on the moon, including on the far side, which no other country has achieved. China aims to land taikonauts on the moon by 2030. It has not signed the Artemis Accords and is building its own set of international partnerships for a separate space station concept. NASA has explicitly described this as a race. Both sides want the lunar south pole. Only one can get there first.

🇷🇺Russia

Russia's story in this race is the most complicated. The Soviet Union was a pioneer. It was first to the moon with robotic spacecraft, first to land a rover, first to return samples. But no Soviet cosmonaut ever walked on the moon. After the USSR collapsed in 1991, Russia's space programme struggled for decades. In August 2023, Russia launched Luna-25, its first lunar mission in 47 years. It crashed on the moon's surface four days before Chandrayaan-3 made a successful landing at almost the same location. Russia plans follow-on missions in 2027 and beyond, now in partnership with China rather than the West. The isolation from European and American space partners following the 2022 invasion of Ukraine has fundamentally changed Russia's position in this race.

🚀SpaceX

SpaceX, the private rocket company, has built the lunar lander for Artemis. Without its lander, there is no Artemis landing. This is genuinely new. In the Apollo era, everything was built by government contractors under government control. Today, a private company holds one of the keys to whether humans land on the moon. The Lunar Gateway, a space station planned to orbit the moon and act as a staging post, was cancelled in March 2026. Plans are being redrawn.

🇮🇳India

India signed the Artemis Accords in June 2023. ISRO's Chandrayaan-3 landed near the lunar south pole in August 2023, the first spacecraft from any country to do so successfully. The data from that mission is shaping Artemis landing site decisions. Chandrayaan-4, planned to return samples from the moon to Earth, is in development. India is also working with NASA on a joint Earth-observation satellite. ISRO's Gaganyaan programme aims to put Indian astronauts in low Earth orbit. India is not a spectator in this race. India is already ahead of several larger space programmes.

Four countries. One destination. The United States (blue), Russia (white), China (red), and India (orange) all have active programmes aimed at the moon's south pole. Each arc represents a space programme, a set of ambitions, and a very large investment of money and national pride. India's arc starts closest to the destination. That is not a coincidence. Illustration: Pip / The Six
India's moment

When Chandrayaan-3 landed on 23 August 2023, scientists at ISRO mission control erupted. Russia's Luna-25 had crashed at almost the same target four days earlier. India's mission cost approximately $75 million. NASA's Artemis I cost approximately $4.1 billion. India reached the south pole of the moon for less than 2% of what Artemis I cost alone.

Russia's complicated history
First to the moon. Never on it.

The Soviet Union sent the first spacecraft to reach the moon in 1959. It sent the first rover to drive on it in 1970. It returned lunar samples three times, robotically. But no Soviet cosmonaut ever walked on the lunar surface. The USSR lost the human spaceflight part of the Space Race. And after 1976, Russia did not try to reach the moon again for 47 years.

Something to think about

If a country lands on the moon and extracts water ice to make rocket fuel, should that resource belong to that country, or to all of humanity? Who should make that decision?

The Artemis Accords

50 countries have signed the Artemis Accords, a framework for peaceful space exploration. The accords include something new: countries can extract and use space resources for their own purposes. The legal framework for a moon economy is being written right now, before the first person has even landed there.

Page 7 — What Next
Pip · The Six
What Next · The Moon and Us 7
What Next

The road ahead — and three things to watch

Artemis II was a test flight. The crew did not land on the moon. They flew around it, checked every system, and proved that Orion can keep humans alive in deep space for ten days. They splashed down in the Pacific on 10 April 2026. If the heat shield performed as expected, the path to Artemis III is open.

Artemis III is the mission that will actually land on the moon. The target is the south pole. For the first time ever, two astronauts will stand on the lunar surface there. One of them is planned to be a woman. It will have been 56 years since the last time any human being stood on the moon.

But the road to Artemis III is not simple. The SpaceX lunar lander still needs to be tested in orbit. The heat shield on Orion needs redesign before it can safely bring a crew back from a full lunar orbit mission. The Lunar Gateway space station, which was supposed to orbit the moon and act as a staging post, was cancelled in March 2026. New plans are being drawn up.

Beyond that, China is aiming for the south pole too. It plans a crewed landing before 2030. Nobody knows which country will get there first. But whoever does will face a question humanity has never had to answer before: who owns the resources inside a permanently shadowed crater, hundreds of thousands of kilometres from Earth, on a world that belongs to no nation?

The moon has been looking down at humanity for 4.5 billion years. For most of that time, we looked back and made stories. For a brief twelve years, we went. Then we stopped and looked again for half a century. Now we are going back. And this time, we plan to stay.

Gene Cernan was the last man to leave the moon. On 14 December 1972, before climbing back into the lunar lander, he scratched his daughter Tracy's initials in the dust. Then he said: "We leave as we came, and God willing as we shall return, with peace and hope for all mankind." Those footprints and those initials are still there. The moon has no wind to blow them away. They will last for millions of years.

The next person to stand on the moon has not yet left Earth. But they are alive today.

Watch this space

Artemis II splashdown, 10 April 2026. If the heat shield holds and the crew return safely, the mission is declared a success and the full Artemis programme moves forward. If problems emerge during re-entry, the timeline for everything that follows will shift.

Chandrayaan-4. India is developing a mission to land on the moon and return samples to Earth. If successful, India becomes only the third country after the US and Soviet Union to bring back material from the moon's surface.

China's crewed timeline. China has publicly stated it aims to land taikonauts on the moon before 2030. Watch for hardware announcements. The gap between the two competing programmes is narrowing faster than most people realise.

Footprints on the moon, still there after more than 50 years. These bootprints were left by Apollo astronauts. The moon has no wind, no rain, no weather of any kind. Nothing disturbs the surface. These prints will remain readable for millions of years, long after every road and building on Earth has crumbled. They are the longest-lasting mark humans have ever made. Photo: NASA / Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter / public domain
Why the moon before Mars?
The rehearsal

Mars is the destination everyone talks about. But the moon is the rehearsal. Living on the moon teaches engineers how to handle radiation, extreme temperatures, dust that destroys equipment, and communication delays. Every hard lesson learned on the moon makes Mars more possible.

Explore: NASA Mars programme

India's next chapter

ISRO's Gaganyaan mission aims to send Indian astronauts to low Earth orbit. Once that succeeds, the question of an Indian crewed lunar mission becomes much easier to ask out loud. India is building the foundations for a human space programme, one mission at a time. The pace is quiet. The direction is clear.

The initials in the dust

Gene Cernan scratched his daughter Tracy's initials, TDC, in the lunar dust before leaving in 1972. The moon has no atmosphere, no wind, no weather. Those initials will remain readable for millions of years. They are the longest-lasting human mark ever made.

Pip

The Six · Edition 2 · April 2026

For curious readers. Always.
